topic: Someone finds out their medical records were mixed up with another patient's
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sterile white of the clinic waiting room suddenly seemed to press in on Clara. She held the file, the official-looking sheaf of papers, feeling the edges crinkle under her anxious grip. A name, a date of birth, a series of diagnostic codes—they were all wrong. Her own history, the very details of her recent procedure, were inexplicably overlaid with those of a woman named Eleanor Vance. Clara’s breath hitched, a shallow, quick intake of air that did nothing to steady the tremor starting in her hands. She looked up, scanning the room, half-expecting someone to notice the unnatural stillness about her. A cold knot tightened in her stomach, making her jaw clench so hard her teeth ached. "This can't be right," she whispered, the sound barely audible over the low hum of the ventilation system. She fumbled for her phone, her fingers clumsy and uncertain as she tried to call the intake desk, the silence on the other end amplifying the sudden, sharp spike of alarm in her chest.

[story 2]

Mark stared at the printed summary, his eyes darting back and forth across the conflicting entries. The ink seemed to swim before his gaze, blurring the lines between what was real and what was a grotesque fabrication. He reread the section detailing his recent blood work, and the information belonged to someone else entirely—a patient he had never encountered. A wave of nausea washed over him, a physical sickness that made his palms slick against the paper. He pushed the document away, the movement jerky, almost violent. He stood up abruptly, needing distance, needing to shake the feeling that his entire reality had just fractured. "Someone needs to check this," he managed to say, his voice tight and strained, the words sounding thin and inadequate in the quiet office. He walked toward the door, his steps heavy and uneven, every muscle in his body coiled tight with a profound sense of disorientation.

[story 3]

When Dr. Evans slid the folder across the desk, Leo felt the blood drain from his face. The discrepancy was glaring, undeniable, a blatant mismatch of medical histories that sent a jolt of pure shock through him. He didn't touch the papers immediately; instead, he leaned back in his chair, his posture rigid, his gaze fixed on the ceiling tiles as if searching for an explanation that simply wasn't there. A strange, buzzing sensation started behind his eyes. He swallowed hard, the dryness in his throat making the action difficult. "Wait," he said, his tone dangerously level, though the slight tremor in his voice betrayed the internal turmoil. He picked up a pen, his hand shaking slightly as he circled the overlapping data points, a desperate need to impose order on the chaos, but finding only deeper confusion.
